Krishna declares that whoever a devotee may be, and whatever form of deity they desire to worship with faith, He makes that specific faith firm and unflinching. Swami Gambirananda's commentary emphasizes that Krishna strengthens "that very firm faith of his." This means that the Supreme Lord doesn't force a particular object of worship but rather amplifies the devotee's existing faith in their chosen path. As A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada notes, God, as the Supersoul, grants independence and facilities for individuals to pursue their desires, including their devotional inclinations, without interference. This divine affirmation of the devotee's chosen path is a key teaching.

question: What is the teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 7, Verse 22 regarding the fulfillment of desires through worship?
answer:
Bhagavad Gita 7.22 states:
सतया श्रद्धया युक्तस्तस्याराधनमीहते |
लभते च ततः कामान्मयैव विहितान्हि तान् ||७-२२||
sa tayā śraddhayā yuktastasyārādhanamīhate .
labhate ca tataḥ kāmānmayaivavihitānhi tān ||7-22||

This verse explains that when a person, endowed with sincere faith (shraddha), worships a particular deity, they indeed obtain their desired objects. However, it's crucial to understand that these desires are *ordained by Me (Krishna) alone*. As Swami Sivananda points out, even when one worships lesser gods or demigods, the fulfillment of desires stems from the Supreme Lord, who alone knows the precise relationship between actions and their results. The demigods themselves operate within the framework and permission granted by the Supreme Lord. Therefore, the ultimate source of all benedictions, even those seemingly granted by other deities, is Krishna Himself. This concept is also echoed in BG 9.23, where Krishna states that even those who worship other deities with devotion are, in fact, worshipping Him, though not in the prescribed way.

question: What is the core teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 7, Verse 23 regarding the results of worship?
answer:
The verse BG 7.23 states:
अन्तवत्तु फलं तेषां तद्भवत्यल्पमेधसाम् |
देवान् देवयजो यान्ति मद्भक्ता यान्ति मामपि ||७-२३||
antavattu phalaṃ teṣāṃ tadbhavatyalpamedhasām .
devāndevayajo yānti madbhaktā yānti māmapi ||7-23||

This verse clearly distinguishes between the fruits of worshipping demigods and the fruits of devotion to the Supreme Lord. It explains that the rewards obtained by those of "small intelligence" (अल्पमेधसाम् - alpamedhasām) who worship minor deities are finite and temporary (अन्तवत् - antavat). The worshippers of the gods go to the gods, achieving temporary heavens or specific planetary systems, as explained by A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, who emphasizes that this leads to different material abodes, not the ultimate spiritual realm. In contrast, Krishna states that His devotees (मद्भक्ता - madbhaktā) come to Him (माम् अपि - māmapi), implying attainment of the eternal, spiritual abode. Swami Chinmayananda further illuminates this by explaining that actions motivated by desires for temporary enjoyments yield impermanent results, much like the quality of a product reflects its source material. This highlights that the ultimate goal of spiritual practice should be the eternal, not the transient. This concept is further reinforced in BG 9.25, which also speaks of the temporary destinations of worshippers of demigods versus the eternal destination of Krishna's devotees.

question: What is Krishna teaching in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 7, Verse 24 about how some people perceive Him?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 7.24, Krishna states:

SANSKRIT: अव्यक्तं व्यक्तिमापन्नं मन्यन्ते मामबुद्धयः |
परं भावमजानन्तो ममाव्ययमनुत्तमम् ||७-२४||

TRANSLITERATION: avyaktaṃ vyaktimāpannaṃ manyante māmabuddhayaḥ |
paraṃ bhāvamajānanto mamāvyayamanuttamam ||7-24||

This verse explains that those who are "abuddhayaḥ" – described by Swami Sivananda as "foolish" and by Dr. S. Sankaranarayanan as "men of poor intellect" – fail to grasp Krishna's true nature. They mistake Him, the Unmanifest and eternal reality, as having a temporary, manifested form, like an ordinary human being who comes into existence. This misunderstanding arises because they are unaware of His "param bhavam" – His higher, immutable, and most excellent nature. As A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada highlights, this ignorance leads them to believe the Supreme Lord ultimately has no form, even when He appears personally. This is akin to mistaking the eternal substratum of reality for the transient phenomena it supports. This ignorance contrasts with the understanding found in verses like BG 9.11, which speaks of those who deride Krishna when He appears in a humanlike form.

question: What is Krishna explaining in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 7, Verse 25 about why He is not fully manifest to everyone?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 7.25, Krishna states:
Sanskrit: नाहं प्रकाशः सर्वस्य योगमायासमावृतः |
मूढोऽयं नाभिजानाति लोको मामजमव्ययम् ||७-२५||
Transliteration: nāhaṃ prakāśaḥ sarvasya yogamāyāsamāvṛtaḥ .
mūḍho.ayaṃ nābhijānāti loko māmajamavyayam ||7-25||

Krishna explains that He is not manifest to all beings because He is veiled by His Yoga-Maya. This "yoga-maya," as Swami Sivananda describes, is His creative illusion, a union of the three gunas of nature that veils the understanding of worldly people. As a result, this deluded world, caught in the cycle of birth and death, fails to recognize Him as the unborn (aja) and imperishable (avyaya) Supreme Being. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada further clarifies that even when Krishna was physically present on Earth, only a select few, His pure devotees, truly understood His divine nature, while others perceived Him as an ordinary human. This is similar to the concept in Chapter 9, Verse 11, where Krishna mentions that foolish men disregard Him when He appears in a human form.

question: What is Krishna's profound statement about His knowledge of time and beings in Bhagavad Gita 7.26?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 7.26, Lord Krishna declares:

वेदाहं समतीतानि वर्तमानानि चार्जुन |
भविष्याणि च भूतानि मां तु वेद न कश्चन ||७-२६||
vedāhaṃ samatītāni vartamānāni cārjuna .
bhaviṣyāṇi ca bhūtāni māṃ tu veda na kaścana ||7-26||

This verse translates to: "I know, O Arjuna, all beings of the past, the present, and the future; but no one knows Me." Krishna asserts His complete omniscience, encompassing all beings across all time. He alone possesses this perfect knowledge.

Swami Sivananda explains that those deluded by material nature's qualities cannot grasp the Lord's true nature. Conversely, A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada highlights that this verse refutes the impersonalistic view, as only a being with a material body would be limited in time and memory. Krishna, being beyond material contamination, transcends these limitations. This ability to know past, present, and future is a testament to His divine nature, a concept echoed in Chapter 4, Verse 6 (BG 4.6), where Krishna states His own eternal, unborn nature.

question: What is the fundamental cause of delusion in all beings at birth, according to Bhagavad Gita Chapter 7, Verse 27?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 7, Verse 27, reveals a profound truth about the origin of delusion in all living beings. The verse states:

SANSKRIT: इच्छाद्वेषसमुत्थेन द्वन्द्वमोहेन भारत |
सर्वभूतानि सम्मोहं सर्गे यान्ति परन्तप ||७-२७||

TRANSLITERATION: icchādveṣasamutthena dvandvamohena bhārata .
sarvabhūtāni sammohaṃ sarge yānti parantapa ||7-27||

This verse explains that at the very moment of creation, or birth, all beings are enveloped by delusion. This delusion arises from the "pairs of opposites" (dvandva), which are fundamentally fueled by desire (icchā) and aversion (dveṣa). As Swami Sivananda explains, desire stems from pleasure and the instinct to preserve the body, while aversion arises from pain. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada further elaborates that this duality of desire and hate blinds us to our true constitutional position as servants of the Supreme Lord, leading us to misunderstand reality. This fundamental entanglement in likes and dislikes, as highlighted by Swami Gambirananda, causes all creatures to become bewildered from their inception, preventing them from realizing the true nature of existence. This concept of entanglement in dualities is a recurring theme, notably in BG 2.14, which discusses the impermanence of material happiness and distress.

question: What is the condition for truly worshipping Krishna, according to Bhagavad Gita Chapter 7, Verse 28?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 7, Verse 28, outlines the qualifications for sincere devotion:

येषां त्वन्तगतं पापं जनानां पुण्यकर्मणाम् |
ते द्वन्द्वमोहनिर्मुक्ता भजन्ते मां दृढव्रताः ||७-२८||
yeṣāṃ tvantagataṃ pāpaṃ janānāṃ puṇyakarmaṇām .
te dvandvamohanirmuktā bhajante māṃ dṛḍhavratāḥ ||7-28||

This verse states that those who have performed virtuous deeds, whose sins have been eradicated, and who are liberated from the delusion of the dualities of life (like pleasure and pain, heat and cold), worship Me with firm resolve. As Swami Sivananda explains, "By the performance of good deeds the heart is slowly purified." This purification is key, as it allows one to transcend the "delusion of the pairs of opposites." This means they no longer get excessively attached to pleasant experiences or repelled by unpleasant ones. Their devotion is unwavering, as highlighted by the phrase "dṛḍhavratāḥ" (steadfast in their vows). This purity of heart and mind is essential for genuine spiritual practice, enabling a stable connection with the Divine, as also seen in the qualities described in BG 12.13-14.

question: What is the ultimate realization for those who take refuge in Krishna and strive for liberation from the cycle of birth and death, as described in Bhagavad Gita 7.29?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 7, Verse 29, states:

SANSKRIT: जरामरणमोक्षाय मामाश्रित्य यतन्ति ये |
ते ब्रह्म तद्विदुः कृत्स्नमध्यात्मं कर्म चाखिलम् ||७-२९||

TRANSLITERATION: jarāmaraṇamokṣāya māmāśritya yatanti ye .
te brahma tadviduḥ kṛtsnamadhyātmaṃ karma cākhilam ||7-29||

This verse reveals that those who take refuge in the Divine (Krishna) and earnestly strive for liberation from the pains of old age and death attain a profound realization. They come to know Brahman, the Absolute Reality, in its entirety. Furthermore, they gain a complete understanding of the Self (adhyātma) and all actions (karma) in their cosmic context. Swami Gambhirananda elaborates that this means they know Brahman as being all individual entities and all actions, signifying a state of complete oneness. This realization transcends the material limitations of the body, as explained in the commentary of Swami Sivananda, where it is stated that such liberated souls are not born again and have conquered old age and death here and now. This concept is echoed in the teaching of the spiritual body being unaffected by material decay, as highlighted by A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada. This ultimate liberation is the culmination of devotional effort and self-knowledge.

question: What is the ultimate realization described in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 7, Verse 30?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 7, Verse 30 states:

Sanskrit: साधिभूताधिदैवं मां साधियज्ञं च ये विदुः |
प्रयाणकालेऽपि च मां ते विदुर्युक्तचेतसः ||७-३०||

Transliteration: sādhibhūtādhidaivaṃ māṃ sādhiyajñaṃ ca ye viduḥ .
prayāṇakāle.api ca māṃ te viduryuktacetasaḥ ||7-30||

This verse describes the profound understanding of the Divine. It explains that those who perceive Me (the Supreme Lord) as being intrinsically connected with the elements of existence (Adhibhuta), the divine forces and deities (Adhidaiva), and the sacrificial principle (Adhiyajna), will, with their minds firmly fixed and concentrated, know Me even at the very moment of their departure from this world. As Swami Gambirananda highlights, this signifies knowing God not just in His transcendent form, but also immanent within the physical and divine realms, and as the essence of all sacrifices. This steadfast knowledge ensures the devotee's continued awareness of the Divine at the crucial time of death, preventing disorientation and leading to a higher destination, as alluded to in BG 8.25 where actions performed at the time of death are emphasized.

question: What are the fundamental concepts Arjuna asks Krishna to clarify in the first verse of Chapter 8 of the Bhagavad Gita?
answer:
In the very beginning of Chapter 8, Arjuna, addressing Krishna as Purushottama (Supreme Person), seeks clarification on several key philosophical terms. The verse states:

अर्जुन उवाच ।
किं तद् ब्रह्म किमध्यात्मं किं कर्म पुरुषोत्तम ।
अधिभूतं च किं प्रोक्तमधिदैवं किमुच्यते ॥८-१॥

arjuna uvāca .
kiṃ tad brahma kimadhyātmaṃ kiṃ karma puruṣottama .
adhibhūtaṃ ca kiṃ proktamadhidaivaṃ kimucyate ||8-1||

Arjuna wants to understand what "Brahman" truly is, the nature of "Adhyatma" (the self or spiritual principle), and the meaning of "Karma" (action). He also inquires about "Adhibhuta" (material nature) and "Adhidaiva" (divine manifestations). As A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada notes, Arjuna is not just asking a friend but the Supreme Authority, seeking to grasp these profound concepts. These questions lay the groundwork for Krishna's subsequent explanations of the ultimate reality and the path to achieving it, as also touched upon in the concept of the Absolute Truth being understood as Brahman, Paramatma, and Bhagavan.

question: What is the meaning of BG 8.2, where Arjuna asks Krishna about "Adhiyajna" and how to know Him at the time of death?
answer:
The verse BG 8.2 reads:
अधियज्ञः कथं कोऽत्र देहेऽस्मिन्मधुसूदन |
प्रयाणकाले च कथं ज्ञेयोऽसि नियतात्मभिः ||८-२||
(adhiyajñaḥ kathaṃ ko.atra dehe.asminmadhusūdana .
prayāṇakāle ca kathaṃ jñeyo.asi niyatātmabhiḥ ||8-2||)

In this verse, Arjuna, addressing Krishna as "Madhusudana" (destroyer of the demon Madhu), seeks clarification on several profound points. He first asks who and in what way the "Adhiyajna" (the Lord of sacrifice) resides within the body. This question stems from the previous chapter's mention of various cosmic principles. Furthermore, Arjuna inquires how the self-controlled, those with disciplined minds, can realize Krishna at the critical moment of death. As Swami Chinmayananda insightfully explains, Arjuna is not merely asking for definitions but is seeking to understand the practical application of spiritual knowledge. This verse, therefore, sets the stage for Krishna to systematically explain these concepts and the path to attaining Him, even at the time of departure from this life, echoing the need for constant remembrance of the Divine as taught in BG 8.7.

question: What is Krishna explaining in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 3 about the nature of reality and action?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 3, Lord Krishna elucidates key philosophical concepts:

śrībhagavānuvāca |
akṣaraṃ brahma paramaṃ svabhāvo.adhyātmamucyate |
bhūtabhāvodbhavakaro visargaḥ karmasaṃjñitaḥ ||8-3||

transliteration: śrībhagavānuvāca . akṣaraṃ brahma paramaṃ svabhāvo.adhyātmamucyate . bhūtabhāvodbhavakaro visargaḥ karmasaṃjñitaḥ ||8-3||

Krishna states that the "Imperishable, the Supreme" is Brahman. Its intrinsic nature, or "self-hood," is referred to as Adhyatma. The act of emission or offering that causes the origin and sustenance of all beings is termed Karma, or action. Swami Sivananda clarifies that Brahman is immutable, eternal, and the source of all existence. He further explains that Adhyatma refers to Brahman as the innermost Self (Pratyagatma) dwelling within each individual. This verse lays the foundation for understanding the ultimate reality and the mechanics of creation. This concept of Brahman as the supreme, immutable reality is also echoed in BG 15.16.

question: What is Lord Krishna explaining about the different aspects of existence in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 4?
answer:
Here's what Lord Krishna reveals in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 4:

अधिभूतं क्षरो भावः पुरुषश्चाधिदैवतम् |
अधियज्ञोऽहमेवात्र देहे देहभृतां वर ||८-४||

Transliteration: adhibhūtaṃ kṣaro bhāvaḥ puruṣaścādhidaivatam .
adhiyajño.ahamevātra dehe dehabhṛtāṃ vara ||8-4||

In this profound verse, Krishna clarifies the nature of three key principles. He states that "adhibhūta" refers to the perishable nature, the ever-changing material world composed of the five elements. "Adhidaivata" is the Purusha, the Supreme Being or the universal soul, from whom all living beings derive their sense power, as explained by Swami Sivananda. Finally, Krishna declares, "I alone am the adhiyajña here in this body, O best among the embodied." This signifies His presence as the Supersoul, the Paramatma, residing within the heart of every individual, overseeing sacrifices and actions. As A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada emphasizes, the word "eva" (alone) highlights the unique role of the Paramatma. This concept of the indwelling divine is also echoed in Chapter 15, Verse 15, where Krishna states, "I am seated in everyone's heart."

question: What is the ultimate teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 5 regarding death and attainment?
answer:
Bhagavad Gita 8.5 states:

Sanskrit: अन्तकाले च मामेव स्मरन्मुक्त्वा कलेवरम् |
यः प्रयाति स मद्भावं याति नास्त्यत्र संशयः ||८-५||

Transliteration: antakāle ca māmeva smaranmuktvā kalevaram .
yaḥ prayāti sa madbhāvaṃ yāti nāstyatra saṃśayaḥ ||8-5||

This verse unequivocally declares that anyone who departs from the physical body at the time of death, while solely remembering Me (Krishna), will attain My divine Being. There is no doubt whatsoever about this. Several translations emphasize the finality and certainty of this promise. Swami Prabhupada highlights the crucial role of Kṛṣṇa consciousness, stating that sincere remembrance is only possible through consistent devotional practice from the beginning of life. Swami Chinmayananda further elaborates that this final remembrance is a culmination of one's life's focus. The verse implies that what occupies the mind at the moment of death is paramount for the soul's subsequent journey. This aligns with the principle found in Chapter 6, Verse 34, which discusses the difficulty of controlling the restless mind, underscoring the importance of cultivating such focused remembrance throughout life.

question: What is the core teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 6 regarding the moment of death?
answer:
The verse BG 8.6 states:
यं यं वापि स्मरन्भावं त्यजत्यन्ते कलेवरम् |
तं तमेवैति कौन्तेय सदा तद्भावभावितः ||८-६||
(yaṃ yaṃ vāpi smaranbhāvaṃ tyajatyante kalevaram |
taṃ tamevaiti kaunteya sadā tadbhāvabhāvitaḥ ||8-6||)

This verse reveals a fundamental principle: the state of consciousness at the time of death profoundly influences one's next destination. As Swami Sivananda and others translate, "Whosoever at the end leaves the body, thinking of any being, to that being only does he go, O son of Kunti (Arjuna), because of his constant thought of that being." In essence, whatever entity or state of being a person is constantly engrossed in and is thinking of at the moment of quitting the physical body, that very being or state they will attain. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada emphasizes that this is not a mere suggestion but a law of nature, illustrated by Mahārāja Bharata's example of being reborn as a deer due to his last thoughts. This principle highlights the importance of cultivating a God-centric consciousness throughout life, as suggested by the concept of *smaran* (remembrance) in verses like BG 8.7.

question: What is Krishna's core instruction to Arjuna in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 7, regarding action and devotion?
answer:
Krishna's profound counsel in this verse is:

SANSKRIT: तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु मामनुस्मर युध्य च |
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्मामेवैष्यस्यसंशयः ||८-७||

TRANSLITERATION: tasmātsarveṣu kāleṣu māmanusmara yudhya ca .
mayyarpitamanobuddhirmāmevaiṣyasyasaṃśayaḥ ||8-7||

This verse instructs Arjuna to constantly remember Krishna while simultaneously performing his prescribed duty, which in his case is to fight. The key is to dedicate one's mind and intellect entirely to the Divine. As Swami Sivananda translates, "With mind and intellect fixed (or absorbed) in Me, thou shalt doubtessly come to Me alone." A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada emphasizes that this instruction is not about renouncing worldly activities but about transforming them by engaging the mind in Krishna consciousness, even while performing one's duties. This dual approach, as highlighted by Swami Chinmayananda, integrates spiritual practice into daily life, elevating both the quality of living and one's overall spiritual progress. By thus acting with unwavering devotion, one is assured of attaining the Divine without doubt. This echoes the principle of Karma Yoga, as also seen in BG 2.47.

question: What is the path to reaching the Supreme Person as described in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 8?
answer:
Bhagavad Gita 8.8 states:

SANSKRIT: अभ्यासयोगयुक्तेन चेतसा नान्यगामिना |
परमं पुरुषं दिव्यं याति पार्थानुचिन्तयन् ||८-८||

TRANSLITERATION: abhyāsayogayuktena cetasā nānyagāminā |
paramaṃ puruṣaṃ divyaṃ yāti pārthānucintayan ||8-8||

This verse reveals a profound method for attaining the Supreme Person. It emphasizes the importance of a mind that is steadfast and unswayed by external distractions. This unwavering focus is achieved through "abhyāsayoga," the yoga of continuous practice. This means consistently meditating on the divine, with the intellect not deviating to any other object. As Swami Sivananda explains, "Abhyasa means practice. Practice is the constant repetition of one idea of God." By diligently cultivating this focused meditation, one eventually reaches the "Paramam Purusham," the Supreme, resplendent Person. This practice is akin to the continuous flow of thoughts towards the Self, as elaborated by Swami Sivananda, leading to a state of deep absorption and ultimate union. This relates to the concept of *smaranam* (remembrance) mentioned by A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, where chanting the Hare Krishna mantra can revive one's memory of Kṛṣṇa and engage the senses and mind towards Him.

question: Can you explain the attributes of the Supreme Being described in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 9 for meditation?
answer:
Certainly. Bhagavad Gita 8.9 offers a profound description of the Supreme Being, highlighting qualities that are conducive to deep meditation. The verse states:

SANSKRIT: कविं पुराणमनुशासितार-
मणोरणीयंसमनुस्मरेद्यः |
सर्वस्य धातारमचिन्त्यरूप-
मादित्यवर्णं तमसः परस्तात् ||८-९||

TRANSLITERATION: kaviṃ purāṇamanuśāsitāraṃ aṇoraṇīyaṃsamanusmaredyaḥ .
sarvasya dhātāramacintyarūpaṃ ādityavarṇaṃ tamasaḥ parastāt ||8-9||

This verse instructs us to meditate on the Supreme as the "Kavi" (Omniscient One), who knows all past, present, and future. He is "Purana" (the Ancient), being the origin of all existence. He is the "Anushasitaram" (the Ruler and Controller), governing the entire universe. He is subtler than the subtlest atom ("anoraṇīyamsam"), yet the "Dhataram" (Supporter) of all beings. His form is "achintyarupam" (inconceivable) to the mundane intellect. He is described as "Adityavarnam" (effulgent like the sun), illuminating all. Finally, He is "tamasaḥ parastat" (beyond the darkness of ignorance).

As Swami Prabhupada insightfully notes, the Lord is a person, and meditating on Him as such, with these attributes, is much easier than contemplating an impersonal void. This emphasis on the personal nature of the Divine is crucial for devotional practice. These attributes help the meditator focus on a concrete, albeit transcendent, reality, leading to a deeper connection. This understanding aligns with the broader theme of devotion discussed in Chapter 12 of the Gita.

question: What is the teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8 Verse 10 regarding the state of mind at the time of death?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 8, Verse 10, describes the state of consciousness required to attain the Supreme Person at the moment of death. The verse states:

SANSKRIT: प्रयाणकाले मनसाऽचलेन
भक्त्या युक्तो योगबलेन चैव |
भ्रुवोर्मध्ये प्राणमावेश्य सम्यक्
स तं परं पुरुषमुपैति दिव्यम् ||८-१०||

TRANSLITERATION: prayāṇakāle manasā.acalena bhaktyā yukto yogabalena caiva .
bhruvormadhye prāṇamāveśya samyak sa taṃ paraṃ puruṣamupaiti divyam ||8-10||

This verse teaches that at the time of departure from the body, one must possess an unshaken mind, be filled with devotion, and utilize the power of yoga. By properly directing the life-breath (prana) to the space between the eyebrows, the individual attains the resplendent Supreme Person. As Swami Chinmayananda insightfully explains, the "time of death" (prayāṇakāle) can also be understood metaphorically as the moment of the death of the ego, when one transcends identification with the body, mind, and intellect. This state is achieved through focused meditation and devotion. This teaching is further elaborated in Chapter 8, Verse 14, which emphasizes remembering Krishna always to achieve Him.

question: What does Krishna mean when He speaks of the "Imperishable Goal" in Bhagavad Gita 8.11?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 8.11, Lord Krishna introduces the ultimate goal of spiritual pursuit, describing it as "imperishable." The verse states:

SANSKRIT: यदक्षरं वेदविदो वदन्ति
विशन्ति यद्यतयो वीतरागाः |
यदिच्छन्तो ब्रह्मचर्यं चरन्ति
तत्ते पदं संग्रहेण प्रवक्ष्ये ||८-११||

TRANSLITERATION: yadakṣaraṃ vedavido vadanti
viśanti yadyatayo vītarāgāḥ |
yadicchanto brahmacaryaṃ caranti
tatte padaṃ saṃgraheṇa pravakṣye ||8-11||

Krishna explains that this is the goal that the knowers of the Vedas declare, which the self-controlled and passion-free ascetics enter into, and for which celibacy is practiced. As Swami Sivananda clarifies, this refers to the Supreme Being, symbolized by the sacred monosyllable Om. Swami Chinmayananda further elaborates that this imperishable goal is the ultimate perfection, and its attainment is facilitated by practices like Omkar (Pranava) worship, which aligns with the Vedic tradition. This same concept of the supreme, unchanging reality is also alluded to in Chapter 13, Verse 12, where Krishna describes the ultimate object of knowledge as the supreme Brahman.

question: Can you explain the profound meditative practice described in Bhagavad Gita 8.12?
answer:
The twelfth verse of the eighth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, BG 8.12, outlines a specific yogic technique for achieving spiritual focus:

**Sanskrit:** सर्वद्वाराणि संयम्य मनो हृदि निरुध्य च |
मूध्न्यार्धायात्मनः प्राणमास्थितो योगधारणाम् ||८-१२||

**Transliteration:** sarvadvārāṇi saṃyamya mano hṛdi nirudhya ca |
mūdhnyā^^rdhāyātmanaḥ prāṇamāsthito yogadhāraṇām ||8-12||

This verse instructs the practitioner to "Having controlled all the passages (gates), confined the mind in the heart, and fixed the life-breath in the head, be established in the practice of concentration." The "gates" refer to the senses of perception and action, which are to be withdrawn from their external objects. The mind, often restless, is then to be held within the heart, symbolizing its redirection inward. Finally, the vital life force, prana, is to be directed and held at the crown of the head, the Brahmarandhra.

Swami Sivananda elaborates that this process involves the practice of *pratyahara* (withdrawal of senses) and then *dharana* (concentration). As A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada notes, this method involves closing the doors of sense enjoyment and focusing the mind on the Supersoul. This practice, while detailed in the Sixth Chapter, is considered challenging in the current age. The ultimate aim is to achieve a state of undisturbed transcendental trance.

question: What is the ultimate goal described in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 13, and how can one achieve it?
answer:
Bhagavad Gita 8.13 reveals a profound method for attaining the Supreme Goal:

ओमित्येकाक्षरं ब्रह्म व्याहरन्मामनुस्मरन् |
यः प्रयाति त्यजन्देहं स याति परमां गतिम् ||८-१३||

omityekākṣaraṃ brahma vyāharanmāmanusmaran .
yaḥ prayāti tyajandehaṃ sa yāti paramāṃ gatim ||8-13||

This verse states that one who departs from the physical body while uttering the single sacred syllable "Om," which represents Brahman (the Absolute Reality), and simultaneously remembering Me (Lord Krishna), achieves the supreme destination. Swami Sivananda clarifies that this involves fixing one's life-breath (Prana) in the crown of the head before leaving the body. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada further emphasizes the significance of chanting, noting that the Hare Krishna mantra, which includes "Om," is recommended for this age and leads to spiritual planets. This practice aligns with the broader teaching in Chapter 6, Verse 10, which speaks of the Yogi who is constantly engaged in meditation and attains the highest perfection.

question: What is the key to easily attaining Krishna, as described in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 14?
answer:
Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 14 reveals a direct path to the Divine:

अनन्यचेताः सततं यो मां स्मरति नित्यशः |
तस्याहं सुलभः पार्थ नित्ययुक्तस्य योगिनः ||८-१४||

*Transliteration:* ananyacetāḥ satataṃ yo māṃ smarati nityaśaḥ .
tasyāhaṃ sulabhaḥ pārtha nityayuktasya yoginaḥ ||8-14||

This verse explains that for a yogi who is constantly and steadfastly devoted, remembering Krishna with an undivided mind, the Lord becomes easily attainable. The emphasis is on *ananyacetāḥ*, meaning a mind that is not attached to or distracted by any other object or desire. As Swami Prabhupada highlights, this describes pure bhakti-yoga where the devotee desires nothing but Krishna. This unwavering focus and continuous remembrance, not just occasional thought, is the crucial element. This concept resonates with the idea of constant remembrance described in BG 6.36, which states that even if one falls short, they should strive for constant remembrance. Through such sustained devotion, the Supreme Lord declares Himself to be easily accessible.

question: What is the ultimate state achieved by great souls who attain Krishna, according to Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 15?
answer:
The verse BG 8.15 states:
मामुपेत्य पुनर्जन्म दुःखालयमशाश्वतम् |
नाप्नुवन्ति महात्मानः संसिद्धिं परमां गताः ||८-१५||
māmupetya punarjanma duḥkhālayamaśāśvatam .
nāpnuvanti mahātmānaḥ saṃsiddhiṃ paramāṃ gatāḥ ||8-15||

This verse explains that those "great souls" (mahatmanah) who have attained the Supreme Being (māmupetya) and achieved the "highest perfection" (saṃsiddhiṃ paramāṃ gatāḥ) are no longer subject to rebirth. This material existence is described as a transient dwelling of suffering (duḥkhālayamaśāśvatam), a cycle of birth, old age, disease, and death. As A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada elaborates, this highest perfection is attained by reaching Krishna's supreme planet, which is beyond material vision. These exalted beings, having reached this ultimate goal, have no desire to return to any of the temporary material worlds, which are inherently filled with misery. This liberation from the cycle of suffering and rebirth is the essence of achieving the highest spiritual realization. This is further echoed in Chapter 9, Verse 35, where Krishna states that even those born of sinful parents attain the supreme destination by taking refuge in Him.

question: What is the ultimate destination Krishna describes in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 16, and how does it differ from other realms?
answer:
ābrahmabhuvanāllokāḥ punarāvartino.arjuna .
māmupetya tu kaunteya punarjanma na vidyate ||8-16||

The verse states: "All the worlds, including the world of Brahma, are subject to return, O Arjuna. But he who reaches Me, O son of Kunti, has no rebirth." This verse highlights a crucial distinction between conditioned existence and liberation. All material realms, even the highest, like Brahmaloka, are temporary and ultimately lead to a return to the cycle of birth and death. As Swami Prabhupada explains, even those who attain Brahmaloka through methods like *pañcāgni-vidyā* will return if they haven't cultivated Kṛṣṇa consciousness there. The true cessation of rebirth, the ultimate goal, is attained only by reaching Krishna's abode, which signifies a complete transcendence of material existence. This contrasts with the concept of *krama-mukti* (gradual liberation) mentioned by Swami Chinmayananda, where one might reach Brahmaloka but still eventually return, unless they achieve realization of the Self and attain the Supreme. This is echoed in BG 9.21, where even those who enjoy heavenly planets return.

question: What is the cosmic scale of time described in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 17?
answer:
The verse BG 8.17 reveals a profound understanding of cosmic time, stating:

SANSKRIT: सहस्रयुगपर्यन्तमहर्यद् ब्रह्मणो विदुः |
रात्रिं युगसहस्रान्तां तेऽहोरात्रविदो जनाः ||८-१७||

TRANSLITERATION: sahasrayugaparyantamaharyad brahmaṇo viduḥ .
rātriṃ yugasahasrāntāṃ te.ahorātravido janāḥ ||8-17||

This verse explains that those who truly understand day and night are those who comprehend the immense duration of a "day of Brahma" and its corresponding "night." Both the day and the night of Brahma are described as lasting for a thousand *yugas* (cosmic ages). As Swami Prabhupada elaborates, a *yuga* cycle (Satya, Treta, Dvāpara, and Kali) is a unit of time, and a thousand of these cycles constitute a single day for Brahma, the creator. This highlights the vastness of material existence and the transient nature of even the highest abodes within it. This concept of cyclical time and cosmic durations is also touched upon in BG 15.16, which differentiates between the perishable and the imperishable.

question: What does Krishna mean when He describes the cycle of manifestation and dissolution in Bhagavad Gita 8.18?
answer:
The verse states:
अव्यक्ताद् व्यक्तयः सर्वाः प्रभवन्त्यहरागमे |
रात्र्यागमे प्रलीयन्ते तत्रैवाव्यक्तसंज्ञके ||८-१८||
(avyaktād vyaktayaḥ sarvāḥ prabhavantyaharāgame |
rātryāgame pralīyante tatraivāvyaktasaṃjñake ||8-18||)

In this verse, Krishna explains the cyclical nature of the universe. He says that all manifested beings and worlds (vyaktayaḥ sarvāḥ) emerge from the Unmanifested (avyaktāt) at the dawn of "day" (aharāgame). Conversely, when "night" (rātryāgame) arrives, these same manifestations dissolve back into that Unmanifested state (tatraivāvyaktasaṃjñake).

Swami Sivananda clarifies that this "day" and "night" refer to the cosmic cycles of creation and dissolution, linked to the awakening and sleep of Brahma, the creator. This concept is further elaborated when Swami Ramsukhdas explains that "vyaktayaḥ" refers to the various bodies of beings. Just as our individual consciousness creates a world of "I" and "mine" upon waking and dissolves it into ourselves when we sleep, the entire manifested cosmos emerges from the subtle nature of Prakriti when Brahma awakens and merges back into it when he sleeps. This cyclical manifestation and dissolution is also alluded to in Bhagavad Gita 9.7 and 9.8, where the Lord states that all beings enter His nature at the end of a cycle and emerge again at the beginning of a new one.

question: What is the teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 19 regarding the cycle of creation and dissolution?
answer:
Bhagavad Gita 8.19 states:

SANSKRIT: भूतग्रामः स एवायं भूत्वा भूत्वा प्रलीयते |
रात्र्यागमेऽवशः पार्थ प्रभवत्यहरागमे ||८-१९||

TRANSLITERATION: bhūtagrāmaḥ sa evāyaṃ bhūtvā bhūtvā pralīyate .
rātryāgame.avaśaḥ pārtha prabhavatyaharāgame ||8-19||

This verse describes the cyclical nature of existence within the material realm. It explains that the same multitude of beings, after being born again and again, is dissolved helplessly at the approach of the "night" and comes forth again at the coming of the "day." As Swami Sivananda clarifies, this "night" and "day" refer to the periods of cosmic dissolution and manifestation. This cycle is not a true annihilation but a temporary merging into an unmanifest state, only to re-emerge with the onset of creation. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada further elaborates that during Brahma's day, beings are manifest and active, but at night, they are annihilated and remain compact within Vishnu's body, only to be manifest again with the arrival of Brahma's day. This emphasizes the impermanent nature of material existence, a concept also touched upon in Chapter 2 when Krishna discusses the soul's eternal nature versus the body's transience (BG 2.20).

question: What is the ultimate reality that transcends even the unmanifested material cause, according to Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 20?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, in Chapter 8, Verse 20, reveals a reality that is beyond even the unmanifested material cause of creation. The verse states:

SANSKRIT: परस्तस्मात्तु भावोऽन्योऽव्यक्तोऽव्यक्तात्सनातनः |
यः स सर्वेषु भूतेषु नश्यत्सु न विनश्यति ||८-२०||

TRANSLITERATION: parastasmāttu bhāvo.anyo.avyakto.avyaktātsanātanaḥ .
yaḥ sa sarveṣu bhūteṣu naśyatsu na vinaśyati ||8-20||

This verse explains that there exists a higher, eternal, unmanifested reality which is distinct from the unmanifested material nature (Avyakta) that serves as the potential cause for all manifested beings. This ultimate reality, the "Eternal Unmanifest," is unchanging and indestructible. Even when all created beings, from the grandest to the smallest, perish, this supreme reality remains untouched. As Swami Sivananda elaborates, this "Para Brahman" is superior to the cosmic creative intelligence (Hiranyagarbha) and the primordial nature because it is their ultimate cause. This concept finds resonance in Chapter 15, Verse 17, which also speaks of the Supreme Being as distinct from the perishable and the imperishable.

question: What is the ultimate goal of spiritual pursuit as described by Krishna in Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 8, Verse 21?
answer:
Here is the verse:
अव्यक्तोऽक्षर इत्युक्तस्तमाहुः परमां गतिम् |
यं प्राप्य न निवर्तन्ते तद्धाम परमं मम ||८-२१||
Transliteration: avyakto.akṣara ityuktastamāhuḥ paramāṃ gatim .
yaṃ prāpya na nivartante taddhāma paramaṃ mama ||8-21||

In this verse, Krishna reveals the highest spiritual destination. He states that the Unmanifested (avyakta) and the Imperishable (akshara) is what the wise identify as the supreme goal. This state, once attained, offers liberation from the cycle of birth and death (samsara), as those who reach it do not return. Krishna further clarifies that this is His own highest abode, His supreme dwelling place or state.

Swami Sivananda elaborates that the Unmanifested is beyond sensory perception, and the Imperishable signifies its all-pervading nature. He emphasizes that this non-dual state is superior to even Brahmaloka. This aligns with the idea that the ultimate goal is not a temporary heavenly realm but a permanent state of being. This teaching is echoed in Chapter 15, Verse 6, where Krishna describes a similar eternal abode from which one never returns.

question: What is the path to attaining the Supreme Person described in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 22?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 8, Verse 22, reveals a profound truth about reaching the ultimate reality. The verse states:

SANSKRIT: पुरुषः स परः पार्थ भक्त्या लभ्यस्त्वनन्यया |
यस्यान्तःस्थानि भूतानि येन सर्वमिदं ततम् ||८-२२||

TRANSLITERATION: puruṣaḥ sa paraḥ pārtha bhaktyā labhyastvananyayā .
yasyāntaḥsthāni bhūtāni yena sarvamidaṃ tatam ||8-22||

This verse explains that the Supreme Person, the highest reality, within whom all beings reside and by whom the entire universe is pervaded, is attainable through unwavering, exclusive devotion. As Swami Sivananda translates, it is "by unswerving devotion to Him alone." This means a devotion that is not divided or distracted by other desires or objects.

Swami Chinmayananda offers a unique insight, emphasizing that this "unalloyed devotion" (अनन्य भक्ति) truly blossoms when the practitioner learns to detach themselves from identification with the body, mind, and intellect. True love for the eternal spiritual truth naturally leads to detachment from the illusory material world. This supreme Person is not merely an abstract concept but the source and sustainer of all existence, as indicated by the phrase "within Whom all beings dwell and by Whom all this is pervaded." This idea is further elaborated in Chapter 9, where Krishna describes how all beings exist within Him and He pervades all. Thus, the path is clear: a singular, steadfast, and loving devotion to the Supreme.

question: What does Krishna mean when He says in Bhagavad Gita 8.23 that He will tell Arjuna about the "times" when yogis return or do not return?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 8.23, Krishna states:

SANSKRIT: यत्र काले त्वनावृत्तिमावृत्तिं चैव योगिनः |
प्रयाता यान्ति तं कालं वक्ष्यामि भरतर्षभ ||८-२३||

TRANSLITERATION: yatra kāle tvanāvṛttimāvṛttiṃ caiva yoginaḥ .
prayātā yānti taṃ kālaṃ vakṣyāmi bharatarṣabha ||8-23||

This verse signifies Krishna's promise to explain the specific times of departure from the physical body that determine a yogi's ultimate destination. As Swami Sivananda translates, "Now I will tell thee, O chief of Bharatas, the times departing at which the Yogis will return or not return." This implies that the timing of death is crucial for those practicing yogic disciplines who are not yet fully liberated. Swami Ramsukhdas offers a profound insight here, explaining that this verse introduces the concept of "krama-mukti" (gradual liberation) and "punaravritti" (return), contrasting them with "sadyo-mukti" (immediate liberation) that was alluded to earlier. Those who depart at certain times may achieve non-return to the cycle of birth and death, while others, depending on their spiritual attainment and the timing of their passing, might return to experience the material world again, perhaps after residing in higher realms. This teaching prepares the ground for understanding the subtle mechanics of spiritual progression beyond the immediate. The concept of timing in departure is also implicitly related to the understanding of when to leave the material world, as discussed in verses concerning the Uttarayana and Dakshinayana paths.

question: What is the teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 24 regarding the path of liberation?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 8, Verse 24, describes a specific path taken by those who have realized Brahman:

SANSKRIT: अग्निर्जोतिरहः शुक्लः षण्मासा उत्तरायणम् |
तत्र प्रयाता गच्छन्ति ब्रह्म ब्रह्मविदो जनाः ||८-२४||

TRANSLITERATION: agnirjotirahaḥ śuklaḥ ṣaṇmāsā uttarāyaṇam .
tatra prayātā gacchanti brahma brahmavido janāḥ ||8-24||

This verse states: "Fire, light, daytime, the bright fortnight, the six months of the northern path of the sun (the northern solstice) – departing then, men who know Brahman go to Brahman." This refers to the Devayana, or the path of the gods, which leads to Brahman. It signifies a journey through auspicious conditions associated with light and the northward movement of the sun. As Swami Sivananda explains, this path leads to salvation and the Brahmaloka. It's important to note, as highlighted by Swami Chinmayananda, that this path is often associated with those who have performed prescribed actions and worships, or those who have attained direct experience of Brahman in this life. This auspicious departure is contrasted with the path of departure during the dark fortnight and the southern solstice, which leads to a return to the cycle of birth and death, as discussed in verse 8.25.

question: What is the teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8 Verse 25 regarding the path of return for a yogi?
answer:
The verse BG 8.25 describes a specific path for yogis that leads to rebirth. It states:

SANSKRIT: धूमो रात्रिस्तथा कृष्णः षण्मासा दक्षिणायनम् |
तत्र चान्द्रमसं ज्योतिर्योगी प्राप्य निवर्तते ||८-२५||

TRANSLITERATION: dhūmo rātristathā kṛṣṇaḥ ṣaṇmāsā dakṣiṇāyanam .
tatra cāndramasaṃ jyotiryogī prāpya nivartate ||8-25||

This verse explains that a yogi who departs from this world during the period characterized by smoke, night, the dark fortnight, and the six months of the southern solstice (Dakshinayana) attains the lunar light (Chandraloka) but eventually returns to this material world. This is often referred to as the Pitriyana, or the path of ancestors, which leads to temporary heavenly abodes. As Swami Sivananda explains, this path is for those who perform sacrifices and charitable works with the expectation of reward. According to A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, those who follow this path may live on the moon for a long time, enjoying life, but their return is inevitable once the fruits of their actions are exhausted. This concept is further elaborated in the context of the departure of those with attachment to worldly results, as alluded to in BG 9.21.

question: What is the meaning of the "bright and dark paths" mentioned in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 26?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 8, Verse 26 states:
śuklakṛṣṇe gatī hyete jagataḥ śāśvate mate |
ekayā yātyanāvṛttimanyayāvartate punaḥ ||8-26||
(śuklakṛṣṇe gatī hyete jagataḥ śāśvate mate |
ekayā yātyanāvṛttimanyayāvartate punaḥ ||8-26||)

This verse describes two eternal paths in the universe: the "bright" (śukla) and the "dark" (kṛṣṇa). The bright path, often referred to as the Northern Path or Devayana, leads to a state of non-return, meaning liberation from the cycle of birth and death. Conversely, the dark path, sometimes called the Southern Path or Pitṛyāna, leads to a return to worldly existence.

Swami Sivananda explains that the bright path is for devotees, while the dark path is for those who perform rituals with the expectation of rewards. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada further elaborates that these paths are taken by fruitive laborers and philosophical speculators who, from time immemorial, are constantly going and coming, never attaining ultimate salvation because they do not surrender to Krishna. This highlights that the ultimate destination is determined by one's spiritual disposition and actions, not merely by the path traversed. This concept is also alluded to in Bhagavad Gita 9.25, which discusses the destinations of those who worship deities versus those who worship the Supreme Lord.

question: What is the ultimate teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 27 regarding spiritual practice?
answer:
BG8.27 —
नैते सृती पार्थ जानन्योगी मुह्यति कश्चन |
तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु योगयुक्तो भवार्जुन ||८-२७||
naite sṛtī pārtha jānanyogī muhyati kaścana .
tasmātsarveṣu kāleṣu yogayukta bhavārjuna ||8-27||

This verse conveys a crucial teaching: by understanding the two distinct paths a soul can take after death – one leading to liberation (the path of light) and the other to continued material existence (the path of darkness) – a yogi is no longer deluded. As Swami Sivananda clarifies, knowledge of these paths acts as a guide, preventing the yogi from losing discrimination. Therefore, the verse urges, "O Arjuna, be steadfast in Yoga at all times." This means maintaining constant connection with the Divine through devotion and service. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada emphasizes that a devotee firmly established in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, constantly engaged in His service, ensures a safe and direct path to the spiritual kingdom, rendering concern over the intermediate paths unnecessary. This constant engagement in devotional yoga is the key to spiritual progress, as also alluded to in Chapter 6, Verse 31, which speaks of the yogi whose mind is absorbed in the Supreme.

question: What is the ultimate benefit for a yogi who truly understands the teachings of the Bhagavad Gita, as described in Chapter 8, Verse 28?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 8, Verse 28, beautifully encapsulates the supreme attainment of a yogi who has internalized the Gita's wisdom. The verse states:

SANSKRIT: वेदेषु यज्ञेषु तपःसु चैव
दानेषु यत्पुण्यफलं प्रदिष्टम् |
अत्येति तत्सर्वमिदं विदित्वा
योगी परं स्थानमुपैति चाद्यम् ||८-२८||

TRANSLITERATION: vedeṣu yajñeṣu tapaḥsu caiva dāneṣu yatpuṇyaphalaṃ pradiṣṭam .
atyeti tatsarvamidaṃ viditvā yogī paraṃ sthānamupaiti cādyam ||8-28||

This verse explains that a yogi who has truly grasped the teachings transcends all the accumulated merit derived from prescribed Vedic rituals, sacrifices, austerities, and charitable deeds. As Swami Sivananda notes, these are declared in scriptures to yield positive results, but the yogi's knowledge elevates them beyond these temporary fruits. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada emphasizes that this verse serves as a culmination of the principles of Kṛṣṇa consciousness and devotional service discussed in the preceding chapters. By understanding the ultimate reality and one's relationship with it, the yogi attains the Supreme Primeval Abode, a state of eternal, unchanging existence. This is also echoed in Verse 6.45, which describes the perfection of the yogi who transcends all material destinations.

question: What is Krishna revealing to Arjuna in the first verse of Chapter 9, and why is it so special?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 9.1, Lord Krishna states:

Sanskrit: श्रीभगवानुवाच |
इदं तु ते गुह्यतमं प्रवक्ष्याम्यनसूयवे |
ज्ञानं विज्ञानसहितं यज्ज्ञात्वा मोक्ष्यसेऽशुभात् ||९-१||

Transliteration: śrībhagavānuvāca .
idaṃ tu te guhyatamaṃ pravakṣyāmyanasūyave .
jñānaṃ vijñānasahitaṃ yajjñātvā mokṣyase.aśubhāt ||9-1||

Krishna declares that He will reveal the "greatest secret" (guhyatamam) to Arjuna, who is receptive and free from fault-finding (anasuyave). This secret is not just theoretical knowledge (jnana), but knowledge combined with direct experience or realization (vijnana). By grasping this, one can achieve liberation from all inauspiciousness or evil (ashubhat). Swami Gambirananda highlights that *jnana* can refer to theoretical knowledge of Brahman from scriptures, while *vijnana* is its direct, experiential realization. This dualistic understanding emphasizes that true liberation comes not just from intellectual comprehension but from profound, lived experience of the Self. This profound teaching is echoed in later verses, such as BG 18.64, which also speaks of the "highest secret."

question: What is the teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 2?
answer:
BG 9.2:
rājavidyā rājaguhyaṃ pavitramidamuttamam .
pratyakṣāvagamaṃ dharmyaṃ susukhaṃ kartumavyayam ||9-2||

This verse profoundly describes the nature of the knowledge of Brahman, often referred to as the knowledge of the Self or the Absolute. Lord Krishna extols it as the "king of sciences" (rājavidyā) and the "king of secrets" (rājaguhyam). It is the supreme purifier (pavitramidamuttamam), capable of cleansing the deepest impurities of the mind and heart.

Crucially, this knowledge is not merely theoretical but is "realizable by direct experience" (pratyakṣāvagamaṃ), meaning it can be directly perceived and intuited. Swami Sivananda emphasizes that there is no blind faith involved, but rather a direct realization. Furthermore, this path is "righteous" (dharmyaṃ), aligning with the principles of Dharma, and "very easy to perform" (susukhaṃ kartum) and "imperishable" (avyayam). As Swami Chinmayananda points out, while conventional religion might involve rituals, this "Dharma" is the art of living an ideal life, scientifically analyzed and understood. This abiding and easily practiced knowledge leads to the imperishable. This echoes the sentiment in BG 2.40, which states that even a little of this Dharma protects one from great fear.

QUESTION: What is the teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 2?
ANSWER:
BG 9.2:
rājavidyā rājaguhyaṃ pavitramidamuttamam .
pratyakṣāvagamaṃ dharmyaṃ susukhaṃ kartumavyayam ||9-2||

This verse highlights the supreme nature of the knowledge of the Absolute, described as the "king of sciences" (rājavidyā) and the "king of secrets" (rājaguhyam). It is the ultimate purifier (pavitramidamuttamam), leading to the highest spiritual attainment. Unlike abstract philosophies, this knowledge is directly perceivable and realizable through intuition and immediate experience (pratyakṣāvagamaṃ), as emphasized by Swami Sivananda, meaning it transcends mere intellectual understanding.

Furthermore, this path is inherently righteous (dharmyaṃ), aligning perfectly with the principles of Dharma, and is described as "very easy to perform" (susukhaṃ kartum) and "imperishable" (avyayam). Swami Chinmayananda sheds light on this by explaining that this "Dharma" is not about rituals but the art of living an ideal life, scientifically analyzed. This profound and accessible wisdom is enduring and leads to an imperishable state, resonating with the idea in BG 2.40 that even a small practice of this Dharma offers great protection.

question: What is the consequence for those who lack faith in the Dharma of spiritual knowledge, as described by Krishna in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 3?
answer:
Krishna states in Bhagavad Gita 9.3:
अश्रद्दधानाः पुरुषा धर्मस्यास्य परन्तप |
अप्राप्य मां निवर्तन्ते मृत्युसंसारवर्त्मनि ||९-३||
aśraddadhānāḥ puruṣa dharmaasy parantapa .
aprāpya māṃ nivartante mṛtyusaṃsāravartmani ||9-3||

This verse explains that individuals who lack faith in this profound Dharma—the knowledge of the Self and the path to realizing the Divine—will not attain Me. Instead, they will inevitably return to the cycle of birth and death, continuing on the mortal pathway of Samsara, which is fraught with suffering and impermanence. Swami Prabhupada emphasizes that faith is the bedrock of spiritual progress, particularly in devotional service. Without this conviction, even with scriptural knowledge, one remains hesitant and unable to progress. This lack of faith, as highlighted by Swami Ramsukhdas, stems from a misplaced trust in transient material possessions and worldly affairs over the eternal truth of the Self. Those who are "regardless of this Dharma" (Swami Gambhirananda) fail to grasp the ultimate reality and remain bound to the perishable world. This leads to a continuous cycle of transmigration, as they haven't found the means to transcend it. This concept is echoed in BG 16.23, where actions performed without faith are described as fruitless.

question: What is the profound teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 4 regarding the nature of the Supreme Being and His relationship with the universe?
answer:
The profound teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 4 is articulated by Krishna as follows:

**Sanskrit:** मया ततमिदं सर्वं जगदव्यक्तमूर्तिना |
मत्स्थानि सर्वभूतानि न चाहं तेष्ववस्थितः ||९-४||

**Transliteration:** mayā tatamidaṃ sarvaṃ jagadavyaktamūrtinā |
matsthāni sarvabhūtāni na cāhaṃ teṣvavasthitaḥ ||9-4||

This verse reveals that the entire universe, in all its manifested forms, is pervaded by Me, the Supreme Being, in My unmanifested aspect. All beings, from the smallest to the largest, find their existence within Me. However, and this is a crucial point, I do not reside within them. As Swami Sivananda explains, this unmanifest form refers to Para Brahman, the Supreme Unmanifested Being, who is imperceptible to the senses but knowable through intuition. While beings exist *through* the Self, which supports everything, the Infinite cannot be contained within the finite. This concept echoes the idea in Chapter 2, Verse 17, which states that the Self pervades all this. Therefore, the relationship is one of pervasive presence and existential dependence, not of containment.

question: What is Krishna teaching us about the relationship between the Supreme Self and all beings in Bhagavad Gita 9.5?
answer:
The verse BG 9.5 states:
न च मत्स्थानि भूतानि पश्य मे योगमैश्वरम् |
भूतभृन्न च भूतस्थो ममात्मा भूतभावनः ||९-५||
na ca matsthāni bhūtāni paśya me yogamaiśvaram .
bhūtabhṛnna ca bhūtastho mamātmā bhūtabhāvanaḥ ||9-5||

Krishna clarifies a potential misunderstanding from the previous verse. While it might seem that all beings exist *within* Him, He reveals His "divine Yoga" or sovereign power. He explains that although He is the sustainer of all beings and the cause of their existence, the beings themselves do not *actually* abide in Him, nor does He dwell within them in a physical sense. As Swami Sivananda points out, Brahman or the Self is formless and attribute-less, making any real connection or dwelling within material forms impossible. Krishna is the efficient cause, bringing forth existence, but He remains distinct and unattached, much like space contains objects but is not contained by them. This concept is echoed in BG 2.17, which states that the Self pervades all existence.

**Related Verse:** BG 2.17

question: What is Krishna teaching us in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 6 about the relationship between beings and Himself?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 6, Lord Krishna reveals a profound truth about His immanent presence and the nature of existence. The verse states:

यथाकाशस्थितो नित्यं वायुः सर्वत्रगो महान् |
तथा सर्वाणि भूतानि मत्स्थानीत्युपधारय ||९-६||

Transliteration: yathākāśasthito nityaṃ vāyuḥ sarvatrago mahān |
tathā sarvāṇi bhūtāni matsthānītyupadhāraya ||9-6||

Krishna explains that just as the mighty wind, which moves everywhere and is vast, is always sustained within the ether (space), similarly, all beings exist within Him. This analogy highlights that while beings are active and seemingly independent, their fundamental existence and movement are entirely dependent on and contained within the Supreme. As Swami Sivananda notes, this illustrates that beings rest in Brahman without any contact or attachment, implying that the Divine is unaffected by the activities of the universe. This concept of the Divine as the substratum of all existence is also seen in Chapter 7, Verse 12, where Krishna states that all manifestations are born from Him.

question: What does Krishna mean when He says in Bhagavad Gita 9.7 that all beings return to His Nature at the end of a cosmic cycle and are sent forth again?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 9, Verse 7 states:

**Sanskrit:** सर्वभूतानि कौन्तेय प्रकृतिं यान्ति मामिकाम् |
कल्पक्षये पुनस्तानि कल्पादौ विसृजाम्यहम् ||९-७||

**Transliteration:** sarvabhūtāni kaunteya prakṛtiṃ yānti māmikām .
kalpakṣaye punastāni kalpādau visṛjāmyaham ||9-7||

In this verse, Krishna explains the cyclical nature of creation and dissolution. He tells Arjuna that at the end of a great cosmic cycle (Kalpa), all beings merge back into His "māmikām prakṛtim" – His own nature, or His material energy. This is a state of dissolution or *Pralaya*. Then, at the beginning of the next cosmic cycle, He sends them forth again, re-manifesting them.

Swami Sivananda elaborates on this by comparing it to natural phenomena: just as grass grows from and returns to the earth, or waves rise from and disappear into the ocean, beings merge into this material nature and are later re-emergent. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada emphasizes that this entire process of creation, maintenance, and annihilation is entirely dependent on the Supreme Lord's will. This concept of cyclical existence is also hinted at in Chapter 8, Verse 18 and 19, which speak of the eternality of the manifest and unmanifested states.

question: What is Krishna's role in the creation and sustenance of the universe as described in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 8?
answer:
Krishna explains His role in creation in Bhagavad Gita 9.8:

SANSKRIT: प्रकृतिं स्वामवष्टभ्य विसृजामि पुनः पुनः |
भूतग्राममिमं कृत्स्नमवशं प्रकृतेर्वशात् ||९-८||

TRANSLITERATION: prakṛtiṃ svāmavaṣṭabhya visṛjāmi punaḥ punaḥ .
bhūtagrāmamimaṃ kṛtsnamavaśaṃ prakṛtervaśāt ||9-8||

This verse reveals that the Supreme Lord, Krishna, actively engages His own nature, Prakriti, to repeatedly manifest the entire universe of beings. He doesn't merely observe; He "animates" or "holds under control" His Prakriti, which is His own energy. This Prakriti, in turn, is the cause of the multitude of beings, who are born "helpless" or "powerless" under its sway.

Swami Gambirananda highlights that this implies Krishna keeps His own Prakriti under control, projecting forth beings who are then subject to the influences of their own nature. This is akin to how the material world is the manifestation of Krishna's inferior energy, as explained by A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, where the Lord enters into creation to facilitate its unfolding. This cyclical process of creation and dissolution is further elaborated in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 8, Verse 19, which speaks of the creation of beings again and again.

question: What does Krishna mean when He says in Bhagavad Gita 9.9 that His actions do not bind Him?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 9.9, Krishna states:

न च मां तानि कर्माणि निबध्नन्ति धनञ्जय |
उदासीनवदासीनमसक्तं तेषु कर्मसु ||९-९||

na ca māṃ tāni karmāṇi nibadhnanti dhanañjaya .
udāsīnavadāsīnamasaktaṃ teṣu karmasu ||9-9||

This verse explains that the cosmic actions of creation and dissolution do not bind Krishna. He remains detached, like one unconcerned or indifferent to these activities. As Swami Sivananda translates, He is "sitting like one indifferent, unattached to those acts." Similarly, Shri Purohit Swami notes that Krishna "remain[s] outside and unattached."

A profound insight here comes from A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, who clarifies that this "neutrality" (udāsīna-vat) doesn't imply inactivity. Krishna is always engaged in His spiritual world. The material activities are carried out by His potencies, and Krishna, while in control, observes them with detachment, much like a high-court judge oversees proceedings without personal involvement in the outcome. This detachment is possible because He does not possess the egoistic sense of being the doer, recognizing the Self as actionless. This concept is further elaborated in the analogy of a bird leaving no trace in the sky, as explained by Swami Chinmayananda, highlighting how selfless actions do not leave binding impressions.

This verse is related to the principle discussed in BG 4.14, where Krishna states that His actions do not bind Him because He is not attached to their fruits.

question: What is the teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 10 regarding the role of nature and the divine in creation?
answer:
Bhagavad Gita 9.10 explains the intricate relationship between the Supreme Lord and material nature in the cosmic creation. The verse states:

SANSKRIT: मयाध्यक्षेण प्रकृतिः सूयते सचराचरम् |
हेतुनानेन कौन्तेय जगद्विपरिवर्तते ||९-१०||

TRANSLITERATION: mayādhyakṣeṇa prakṛtiḥ sūyate sacarācaram .
hetunānena kaunteya jagadviparivartate ||9-10||

This verse clarifies that under the Lord's supervision, material nature (Prakriti) gives birth to all movable and immovable beings. As Swami Sivananda explains, the Lord acts as a supervisor or witness, and it is through His proximity or presence that Nature functions. While Nature is the primary cause of creation, the Lord's presence is essential for its operation. Swami Ramsukhdas draws an analogy to electricity powering various machines; similarly, the Lord's power animates Prakriti. This dynamic ensures the constant flux and cyclical nature of the universe. This concept is echoed in Chapter 11, Verse 32, where Krishna reveals His cosmic form as Time, the destroyer of worlds, highlighting His ultimate control over all transformations.

question: What is Krishna teaching us in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 11 about how some people perceive Him?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 9.11, Lord Krishna states:

SANSKRIT: अवजानन्ति मां मूढा मानुषीं तनुमाश्रितम् |
परं भावमजानन्तो मम भूतमहेश्वरम् ||९-११||

TRANSLITERATION: avajānanti māṃ mūḍhā mānuṣīṃ tanumāśritam .
paraṃ bhāvamajānanto mama bhūtamaheśvaram ||9-11||

This verse reveals that "Fools disregard Me, dwelling in a human form, not knowing My higher nature, as the Supreme Lord of all beings." (Swami Adidevananda). Many are deluded and fail to recognize the divine within the human guise, akin to mistaking a mirage for the Ganga. As Swami Sivananda explains, this lack of discrimination prevents them from seeing Krishna's true, higher Being, which is the great Lord of all existence. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada emphasizes this point by noting that the Lord, who orchestrates creation, maintenance, and annihilation, cannot be an ordinary human, despite His appearance. This ignorance leads them to treat the Supreme with disrespect. This is further illuminated in Chapter 4, Verse 6, where Krishna declares His own unborn, imperishable, and divine nature.

question: What is the meaning of Bhagavad Gita 9.12, and who are the people described in this verse?
answer:
The twelfth verse of the ninth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, moghāśā moghakarmāṇo moghajñānā vicetasaḥ | rākṣasīmāsurīṃ caiva prakṛtiṃ mohinīṃ śritāḥ ||9-12||, describes individuals who are fundamentally misguided. Krishna explains that these individuals are characterized by "vain hopes, vain actions, vain knowledge, and are senseless." They are possessed of a "deceitful nature," aligning with the demonic (rākṣasī) and undivine (āsurī) dispositions.

Swami Sivananda elaborates that their hopes are vain because they chase transient, perishable objects, neglecting the eternal. Their actions are futile as they are not performed as a sacrifice to the Lord. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada further clarifies that those who mock or do not accept Krishna as the Absolute Truth, even if performing pious activities, will not achieve the ultimate goal of returning to Godhead. This verse serves as a stark warning against a materialistic or atheistic outlook that obstructs spiritual progress, echoing the sentiment in BG 7.23 where temporary results are mentioned for those who worship demigods.

question: What is the teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 13 regarding those who worship God with a pure heart?
answer:
Bhagavad Gita 9.13 describes the nature of the truly elevated souls, the *mahatmas*. The verse states:

SANSKRIT: महात्मानस्तु मां पार्थ दैवीं प्रकृतिमाश्रिताः |
भजन्त्यनन्यमनसो ज्ञात्वा भूतादिमव्ययम् ||९-१३||
TRANSLITERATION: mahātmānastu māṃ pārtha daivīṃ prakṛtimāśritāḥ .
bhajantyananyamanaso jñātvā bhūtādimavyayam ||9-13||

This verse teaches that those with great souls, O Arjuna, taking refuge in the divine nature, worship Me with an undivided mind, knowing Me as the imperishable source of all beings. This means they have embraced spiritual qualities and have surrendered to the divine, freeing themselves from material nature's influence. As Swami Prabhupada highlights, this surrender is the key qualification, placing the devotee under the guidance of spiritual nature. This unwavering devotion stems from the realization of God's infinite, unchanging essence, as also alluded to in Chapter 7, Verse 2, which describes the attainment of divine nature through devotion.

question: What does Krishna mean when He describes the devotees who worship Him in Chapter 9, Verse 14?
answer:
Krishna elaborates on the nature of His devoted worshippers in this profound verse:

सततं कीर्तयन्तो मां यतन्तश्च दृढव्रताः |
नमस्यन्तश्च मां भक्त्या नित्ययुक्ता उपासते ||९-१४||
satataṃ kīrtayanto māṃ yatantaśca dṛḍhavratāḥ |
namasyantaśca māṃ bhaktyā nityayuktā upāsate ||9-14||

This verse describes devotees who are constantly glorifying the Lord, striving with firm resolve, paying homage to Him, and who are perpetually united with Him in devotion. They are not merely performing outward rituals, but are deeply engaged in remembering and praising His divine name, qualities, and pastimes. As Swami Sivananda notes in his commentary, this includes practices like Japa, studying scriptures, and cultivating virtues such as patience and truthfulness. A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada emphasizes that this constant glorification signifies an attachment to the Supreme Personality of Godhead Himself, not just His impersonal aspect. This steadfast devotion is a continuous engagement, as highlighted by the term "nityayuktāḥ" (always steadfast), indicating a profound and unwavering connection to the Divine. This echoes the sentiment in BG 12.8, where Krishna states that for those whose minds are fixed on Him, He becomes the swift deliverer from the ocean of birth and death.

question: What does Krishna mean in Bhagavad Gita 9.15 when He describes various ways people worship Him?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 9.15, Krishna states:

"ज्ञानयज्ञेन चाप्यन्ये यजन्तो मामुपासते |
एकत्वेन पृथक्त्वेन बहुधा विश्वतोमुखम् ||९-१५||"
(jñānayajñena cāpyanye yajanto māmupāsate .
ekatvena pṛthaktvena bahudhā viśvatomukham ||9-15||)

This verse explains that besides other forms of worship, some devotees worship Krishna through the "sacrifice of knowledge" (jñānayajñena). They adore Him in various ways: as One, as distinct, and as manifold, recognizing Him as the All-faced or Universally-faced Being. As Swami Sivananda points out in his commentary, this can involve seeing the Self in all, or perceiving all forms and sounds as divine manifestations. Some worship Him monistically, realizing the oneness of the Self with Brahman, while others worship Him in His diverse cosmic forms. This understanding aligns with the idea that the Supreme can be approached through different perspectives based on the devotee's realization. This concept is also touched upon in Chapter 6, Verse 30, where Krishna says that He is present in all beings and that those who see Him everywhere worship Him.

question: What is Krishna trying to convey in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 16 about His omnipresence?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 9.16, Krishna declares His all-encompassing nature through a series of sacrificial elements:

अहं क्रतुरहं यज्ञः स्वधाहमहमौषधम् |
मन्त्रोऽहमहमेवाज्यमहमग्निरहं हुतम् ||९-१६||

ahaṃ kraturahaṃ yajñaḥ svadhāhamahamauṣadham .
mantro.ahamahamevājyamahamagnirahaṃ hutam ||9-16||

Krishna states, "I am the Kratu (a type of Vedic sacrifice), I am the Yajna (worship or sacrifice), I am the Svadha (offering to ancestors), I am the Aushadha (herbs and medicinal plants), I am the Mantra (sacred hymn), I am the Ajya (clarified butter), I am the Agni (fire), and I am the Hutam (oblation)."

This verse highlights Krishna's immanence in all aspects of Vedic ritual and worship. As Swami Sivananda explains, "Kratu is a kind of Vedic sacrifice... Yajna is the worship enjoined in the Smriti." By identifying Himself with each component, from the sacred chants to the fire and the offerings, Krishna emphasizes that the entire process, and indeed the underlying divine principle, is none other than Himself. This echoes the idea from Chapter 4, Verse 24, where even the act of offering in the fire is described as Brahman. This profound declaration illustrates that devotion is not limited to specific acts but is a recognition of the divine in all things.

question: What is Krishna revealing about His nature and relationship to the universe in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 17?
answer:
BG 9.17: पिताहमस्य जगतो माता धाता पितामहः |
वेद्यं पवित्रमोंकार ऋक्साम यजुरेव च ||९-१७||
Transliteration: pitāhamasya jagato mātā dhātā pitāmahaḥ .
vedyaṃ pavitramoṃkāra ṛksāma yajureva ca ||9-17||

In this profound verse, Krishna declares His all-encompassing relationship with the cosmos. He is not just a distant deity, but the very source of existence – the father, mother, sustainer (dhātā), and even the grandfather of the universe. This signifies that He is the ultimate cause and sustainer of all beings. Furthermore, He reveals Himself as the ultimate object of knowledge (vedyam), the purifier, the sacred monosyllable Om, and the essence of the foundational Vedic hymns (Rik, Sama, and Yajur).

Swami Chinmayananda offers a unique insight here, emphasizing that by detailing these familial and foundational roles, Krishna is dispelling any notion of Him being an abstract, impersonal Absolute. Instead, He is presented as the Supreme Being, intimately connected and the ultimate object of love and devotion. This concept of Krishna as the sole knowable reality, whose realization leads to the cessation of worldly suffering, is a recurring theme, echoing the teachings found in verses like BG 7.19.

question: What does Krishna mean when He lists so many attributes for Himself in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 18?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 9.18, Krishna elaborates on His all-encompassing nature by listing numerous roles He plays in the universe. The verse states:

गतिर्भर्ता प्रभुः साक्षी निवासः शरणं सुहृत् ।
प्रभवः प्रलयः स्थानं निधानं बीजमव्ययम् ॥९-१८॥
gatirbhartā prabhuḥ sākṣī nivāsaḥ śaraṇaṃ suhṛt .
prabhavaḥ pralayaḥ sthānaṃ nidhānaṃ bījamavyayam ||9-18||

Krishna declares, "I am the goal, the supporter, the Lord, the witness, the abode, the shelter, the friend, the origin, the dissolution, the foundation, the treasure-house, and the imperishable seed." This multifaceted description reveals that He is the ultimate destination of all beings, the sustainer of all existence, the supreme controller, and the silent observer of all actions. He is the dwelling place for all, the refuge for the distressed, and the benevolent friend who acts without expectation of return. He is the source of creation, the point of dissolution, the stable foundation, the repository of all treasures, and the immutable seed from which everything emanates. As Swami Prabhupada explains, "Gati means the destination where we want to go. But the ultimate goal is Kṛṣṇa." This signifies that all paths, in their ultimate realization, lead back to Him. This comprehensive portrayal underscores His immanence and transcendence, connecting Him to every aspect of reality, much like the concept of Brahman described in the Upanishads. This is further echoed in Chapter 10, Verse 8, where Krishna states, "I am the source of all spiritual and material worlds. Everything proceeds from Me."

question: What does Krishna mean when He says in Bhagavad Gita 9.19 that He is heat, rain, immortality, death, existence, and non-existence?
answer:
**Sanskrit:** तपाम्यहमहं वर्षं निगृह्णाम्युत्सृजामि च |
अमृतं चैव मृत्युश्च सदसच्चाहमर्जुन ||९-१९||

**Transliteration:** tapāmyahamahaṃ varṣaṃ nigṛhṇāmyutsṛjāmi ca .
amṛtaṃ caiva mṛtyuśca sadasaccāhamarjuna ||9-19||

In this profound verse, Krishna reveals His immanent presence in the cosmic processes. He explains that He is the very force that radiates heat (like the sun), and He controls the cycle of rain – withholding it and sending it forth. Beyond these natural phenomena, He asserts His identity with the ultimate dualities of existence: immortality and death, as well as being (sat) and non-being (asat).

Swami Sivananda's commentary highlights that "Sat" refers to the manifested world, the effect, while "Asat" signifies the unmanifested cause. It's crucial to understand that this "non-existence" does not imply absolute nothingness, but rather the subtle, unmanifested state from which existence arises. This concept is further elaborated in Chapter 11, Verse 32, where Krishna declares, "I am Time, the great destroyer of the worlds, and I have come here to destroy all the people." This demonstrates how Krishna, as Time, encompasses even the apparent annihilation that leads to new creation. By encompassing these apparent opposites, Krishna underscores His supreme nature as the source and sustainer of all that is and all that is not.

question: What is the teaching of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 20 regarding those who follow Vedic rituals for heavenly rewards?
answer:
The Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 9, Verse 20, describes a specific category of individuals who engage in Vedic practices:

SANSKRIT: त्रैविद्या मां सोमपाः पूतपापा
यज्ञैरिष्ट्वा स्वर्गतिं प्रार्थयन्ते |
ते पुण्यमासाद्य सुरेन्द्रलोक-
मश्नन्ति दिव्यान्दिवि देवभोगान् ||९-२०||

TRANSLITERATION: traividyā māṃ somapāḥ pūtapāpā yajñairiṣṭvā svargatiṃ prārthayante .
te puṇyamāsādya surendralokaṃ aśnanti divyāndivi devabhogān ||9-20||

This verse explains that those who are learned in the three Vedas (Rig, Sama, and Yajur), have purified themselves by drinking Soma juice, and worship the Divine through sacrifices, often pray for passage to heaven. Upon reaching the virtuous world of Indra, the king of the gods, they enjoy celestial pleasures. Swami Prabhupada highlights that these "traividyāḥ" are often attached to the knowledge derived from the Vedas but may miss the ultimate purpose, which is devotion to Krishna. While these rituals lead to temporary heavenly enjoyment, they are still within the cycle of birth and death. This verse implicitly contrasts such actions with the path of pure devotional service, which leads to eternal liberation. This is also alluded to in Chapter 2, Verse 42, where Krishna describes those who are overly attached to Vedic ritualistic injunctions for material enjoyment.

question: What does Krishna mean in Bhagavad Gita 9.21 when He talks about "going and returning" for those who follow Vedic rituals with desires?
answer:
In Bhagavad Gita 9.21, Krishna explains the cyclical nature of existence for those who perform Vedic rituals with worldly desires. The verse states:

SANSKRIT: ते तं भुक्त्वा स्वर्गलोकं विशालं
क्षीणे पुण्ये मर्त्यलोकं विशन्ति |
एवं त्रयीधर्ममनुप्रपन्ना
गतागतं कामकामा लभन्ते ||९-२१||

TRANSLITERATION: te taṃ bhuktvā svargalokaṃ viśālaṃ
kṣīṇe puṇye martyalokaṃ viśanti |
evaṃ trayīdharmamanuprapannā
gatāgataṃ kāmakāmā labhante ||9-21||

This verse means that individuals who perform rituals prescribed by the three Vedas, seeking heavenly enjoyments, do attain those vast celestial realms. However, their stay is temporary. Once the accumulated merit (punya) that earned them their celestial residence is exhausted, they are reborn into the mortal world. Krishna highlights that this is a cycle of "going and returning" (gatāgataṃ) for those who are driven by desires (kāmakāmā). As Swami Prabhupada elaborates, this is like being on a Ferris wheel, ascending to higher planets and then descending back to Earth, failing to achieve the ultimate goal of life, which is liberation from this cycle. This contrasts with the spiritual path of understanding Krishna, the cause of all causes, as mentioned in the Vedanta-sutra (janmādy asya yataḥ), which leads to permanent transcendence.

question: What is Krishna promising to His devotees in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 9, Verse 22?
answer:
Bhagavad Gita 9.22 states:
अनन्याश्चिन्तयन्तो मां ये जनाः पर्युपासते |
तेषां नित्याभियुक्तानां योगक्षेमं वहाम्यहम् ||९-२२||
ananyāścintayanto māṃ ye janāḥ paryupāsate .
teṣāṃ nityābhiyuktānāṃ yogakṣemaṃ vahāmyaham ||9-22||

In this verse, Lord Krishna makes a profound promise to those who are exclusively devoted to Him. It means that for those who worship Him alone, without any other thought or aspiration, and who are constantly united with Him, Krishna personally takes responsibility for their well-being. He assures them that He will secure what they lack (yoga) and preserve what they already possess (kshema). Swami Sivananda elaborates that "yoga" here refers to the acquisition of spiritual attainments and "kshema" to their preservation. This divine assurance highlights the power of unwavering devotion. This concept resonates with Krishna's promise in Chapter 18, Verse 66, where He instructs Arjuna to surrender to Him alone, assuring liberation.

question: What is Krishna's message in Bhagavad Gita 9.23 regarding the worship of other deities?
answer:
The verse states:
येऽप्यन्यदेवता भक्ता यजन्ते श्रद्धयान्विताः ।
तेऽपि मामेव कौन्तेय यजन्त्यविधिपूर्वकम् ॥९-२३॥
ye.apyanyadevatābhaktā yajante śraddhayānvitāḥ .
te.api māmeva kaunteya yajantyavidhipūrvakam ||9-23||

In Bhagavad Gita 9.23, Krishna explains that even those who worship other deities with faith are, in essence, worshiping Him alone. However, their method of worship is considered "avidhipurvakam" – not in accordance with the prescribed injunctions or the right way.
